<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272736221 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 03195: (2026x) Humidifier example to Simulink model R2016a

- page_id: `272736221`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272736221/2026x+Humidifier+example+to+Simulink+model+R2016a
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:46:53.952+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x) Exporting to External Simulation Models > (2026x) Simulink export > (2026x) Simulating exported Simulink models > (2026x) Humidifier example to Simulink model
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To generate a Simulink model directly from the modeling tool

1. Right-click the Block TestCaseHumidifier::HumidifierSystem and select Tools > Export to Simulink.
2. Set the options listed below and click OK :
  - **Format**: XML (.sdl)
  - **S-Function or Simscape**: S-Function version 2
3. Launch Matlab with Simulink and Simscape extensions.
4. In Matlab’s Current Directory navigation bar, search for the file directory where the Simulink files (as well as the SysML v1 files) are saved, and set it as the current directory. The Current Folder panel should display two generated Simulink “.slx” files. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. In the Current Folder panel, double-click on the file named Environment.slx . The model will open up in a Simulink window. Rearrange the blocks to reflect the signal flow arrowhead directions. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. Open the Library Browser either by using the 4-block icon or by going to Tools > Library Browser . Find and click on Sinks among the list in the Libraries panel, which will be categorized under Simulink . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. Pull-and-drag the Scope block from the Sinks library list into the Environment Simulink model. Attach a signal line segment from the Scope port onto the signal line that connects the humidity-out port of the room block to the humidity-in port of the humidifiersystem block. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Go to Simulation > Model Configuration Settings . This allows selecting the types of solver and the runtime of the simulation. Select a Start Time of 0 , and a Stop Time of 3600 . Under Solver Options , select the solver Type to Variable-step (or any other desirable solver that is suitable) and change the Max step size to 0.01 . Press Apply , then press OK . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
9. Double-click the Scope block in the model. An empty (black) plot will show up. Press the ‘Scope’ parameters button (the gear icon above the empty plot). A ‘Scope’ parameters dialogue box will pop up. Go to the History tab and uncheck the option box Limit data points to last . Press Apply , and then OK . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
10. Go to Simulation > Run . Double-click on the Scope block to see the simulation results. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Attach a signal line segment from the <strong>Scope</strong> port onto the signal line that connects the<em> humidity-out</em> port of the room block to the<em> humidity-in</em> port of the<strong> humidifiersystem</strong> block.<br /><br /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:width="700"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Attaching_a_Scope_Block.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Humidifier example to Simulink model R2016a" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></li><li>Go to <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Model Configuration Settings</strong>. Press<strong> Apply</strong>, then press<strong> OK</strong>.<br /><br /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:width="750"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Simulink_Model_Configuration_Settings.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Humidifier example to Simulink model R2016a" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></li><li>Double-click the <strong>Scope</strong> block in the model. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272733038 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 03196: (2026x) Humidifier System example

- page_id: `272733038`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272733038/2026x+Humidifier+System+example
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:41:44.233+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x) Exporting to External Simulation Models > (2026x) Modelica export > (2026x) Simulating exported Modelica models
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page describes how to simulate the *Humidifier*example model in Dymola. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Humidifier system sample' space='']Learn more about *Humidifier*sample model >>

You can find the *Humidifier*sample model in:

- the modeling tool: Welcome window > Samples > Simulink and Modelica Transformation > Humidifier.
- the Installation directory: <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML v1\Simulink and Modelica Transformation\Humidifier.

To simulate the *Humidifier System* model

1. Export the HumidifierSystem Block to the Modelica file. To learn more, refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Modelica export' space=''] page.
2. Download and install Dymole in your system. To learn more, refer to the Dymola page.
3. Launch the Dymola.
4. Click File > Open > Load... and select the saved *HumidifierSystem**.mo* file from your directory. The Humidifier System model is displayed in the **Package Browser**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. In the Graphics tab toolbar, click Check to check for syntax and semantic errors in the model. The Logs panel displays whether the model is ready for simulation. [ATTACHMENT filename='Check Button.png']
6. Setup the simulation run for the model: 
 a. Select the******Simulation**tab and in its toolbar click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. 
b. In the **Simulation Setup** dialog, specify the **Start time******value to **0** and the **Stop time******value to**3600**seconds. 
c. Click **OK**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. In the**Simulation** tab toolbar, click **Simulate**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] When the simulation is completed, the output is displayed in the panel at the bottom and the *HumidifierSystem*variables are displayed in the **Variable Browser**.
8. To see the input and output of the EnvironmentModel , variables from the Variable Browser must be chosen. Selecting EnvironmentModel > _Environment > room > humidity_out will display the humidity level of the room overtime on the plotting window.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The <strong>Logs </strong>panel displays whether the model is ready for simulation.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Check Button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Humidifier System example" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Setup the simulation run for the model:<br />    a. Select the<strong> </strong><strong>Simulation </strong>tab and in its toolbar click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="21"><ri:attachment ri:filename="setup_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Humidifier System example" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    b. In the <strong>Simulation Setup</strong> dialog, specify the <strong>Start time</strong><strong> </strong>value to <strong>0</strong> and the <strong>Stop time</strong><strong> </strong>value to<strong> 3600 </strong>seconds.</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    c. Click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Simulatio Setup.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Humidifier System example" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></span></span></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the<strong> Simulation</strong> tab toolbar, click <strong>Simulate</strong>. <br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Simulate Button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Humidifier System example" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c0ad6689-6aa0-43a3-b678-89d3d32e2be3"><ac:rich-text-body><p>When the simulation is completed, the output is displayed in the panel at the bottom and the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><em>HumidifierSystem </em>variables are displayed in the <strong>Variable Browser</strong>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To see the input and output of the</span><strong> EnvironmentModel</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">, variables from the </span><strong>Variable Browser</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> must be chosen. Selecting </span><strong>EnvironmentModel</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> &gt; </span><strong>_Environment</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> &gt; </span><strong>room</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> &gt; </span><strong>humidity_out</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> will display the humidity level of the room overtime on the plotting window.</span></li></ol><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272733045 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 03198: (2026x) Hydraulics example

- page_id: `272733045`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272733045/2026x+Hydraulics+example
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-11-25T14:42:00.405+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x) Exporting to External Simulation Models > (2026x) Modelica export > (2026x) Simulating exported Modelica models
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa', 'unrestored-unknown-attachment']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page describes how to simulate the *Hydraulics* example model in Dymola. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Hydraulics sample' space='']Learn more about *Hydraulics*sample model >>

You can find the *Hydraulics*sample model in:

- the modeling tool: Welcome window > Samples > Simulink and Modelica Transformation > Hydraulics .
- the Installation directory: <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML v1\Simulink and Modelica Transformation\*Hydraulics**.*

To simulate the *ConnectedTanks* model

1. Export the ConnectedTanks Block to Modelica file. How to >>
2. Make sure the Dymola tool is installed. How to >>
3. Start the Dymola.
4. Click File > Open > Load... and select saved ConnectedTanks .mo file from your file directory. A ConnectedTanks model is displayed in the Package Browser . [ATTACHMENT filename='connectedtanks_model_in_package_browser.png']
5. In the Graphics tab toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='check_syntax_button.png'] to check model for syntax and semantic errors. The result panel displays whether the model is ready for simulation. [ATTACHMENT filename='check_syntax_connectedTanks.png']
6. Setup the simulation run for the model: a. Select the Simulation tab and in its toolbar click [ATTACHMENT filename='setup_button.png'] . b. In the Simulation Setup dialog, specify the Start time value to 0 and the Stop time value to 120 seconds (or any other reasonable amount of time; for this model, 120 seconds should be enough) . c. Click OK . [ATTACHMENT filename='simulation_setup_connectedTanks.png']
7. In the**Simulation** tab toolbar, click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. When the simulation is completed, the output is displayed in the panel at the bottom and the *ConnectedTanks***variables are displayed in the **Variable Browser**.
8. To see the input and output of the ConntectedTanks model , select variables in the**Variable Browser**as follows: - ConnectedTanks > _ConnectedTanks > fluidReservoir1 > fluidLevel . The changing fluid level of the first tank is displayed on the **Plot** window. See the following figure. - ConnectedTanks > _ConnectedTanks > fluidReservoir2 > fluidLevel . Thechanging fluid level of the second tank is displayed on the **Plot**window. See the following figure. [ATTACHMENT filename='plot_diagram_fluid_level.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272733066 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 03199: (2026x) Hydraulics example to Simulink and Simscape model

- page_id: `272733066`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272733066/2026x+Hydraulics+example+to+Simulink+and+Simscape+model
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-11-25T14:41:51.334+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x) Exporting to External Simulation Models > (2026x) Simulink export > (2026x) Simulating exported Simulink models
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page describes how to simulate the *Hydraulics* example modelin Simulink. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Hydraulics sample' space='']Learn more about *Hydraulics*sample model >>

You can find the *Hydraulics*sample model in:

- the modeling tool: Welcome window > Samples > Simulink and Modelica Transformation > Hydraulics .
- the Installation directory: <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML v1\Simulink and Modelica Transformation\*Hydraulics**.*

To simulate the *Hydraulics* model

1. Export the ConnectedTanks Block to Simulink file. How to >>
2. In the Simulink Export Options dialog select the following options:
  - **Format**: XML (.slx)
  - **S-Function or Simscape**: Simscape
  - Simscape port libraries : Create new port types
  - Composite Signals : Bus Creators/Selectors
3. Make sure the MATLAB tool is installed.
4. Double-click the MATLAB icon to start it.
5. In the file directory select the location of your exported *Hydraulics* model Simulink files and choose the +ConnectedTanksLibrary folder. The simscape files (.ssc) are displayed in the Current Folder panel. [ATTACHMENT filename='file_directory_simulink_files_connectedTanks.png'] ssc_build
6. In the Command Window type ssc_build (or sscbuild depending on the Matlab version) and press Enter . This generates a folder sscprj in the +**ConnectedTanksLibrary** directory as well as a **ConnectedTanksLibrary**_lib.slx file in the directory with the original ConnectedTanks .slx file. [ATTACHMENT filename='command_window_connectedTanks.png']
7. Go back to the file directory, select the Hydraulics package with exported Simulink files and in the Current Folder select the ConnectedTanks.slx . [ATTACHMENT filename='current_folder_connectedTanks.png']
8. In the Current Folder panel, double-click on ConnectedTanks .slx to open the Simulink/Simscape model. Rearrange the blocks to get a better picture of the blocks and links. [ATTACHMENT filename='hydraulics_model_simscape.png']
9. On the **SIMULATION** tab, click the arrow on the **PREPARE** area and select the **Model Settings**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
10. In the **Configuration Parameters** dialog, on the left side of the dialog: 
a. Select a **Solver**property group****and specify the following on the right side of the dialog: 
****-****In the the **Simulation time** group, change **Start time**to **0.0**, **Stop time**to **200**(or any other reasonable number of seconds). 
 - In the **Solver selection** group, select**Type****Variable-step** or any other desirable solver that is suitable. 
 - Click the arrow of the **Solver details** group and change the **Max step size** to **0.01**. b. Select Simscape property group and specify the following on the right side of the dialog: - In the Data Logging group, change Log simulation data to All . - Make sure Limit data points is unchecked. [ATTACHMENT filename='configuration_parameters_dialog_connectedTanks.png']
11. Press Apply > OK .
12. In the ConnectedTanks Simulink window, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''].
13. On the main toolbar, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to open a Simscape Results Explorer : ConnectedTanks window where you can expand the ConnectedTanks blocks to see how their properties simulated through time. For the tanks fluidReservoir1 and fluidReservoir2 , the properties fluidLevel can be selected to see how the fluid reacted transferred from one tank to the other in the simulation. To see the simulation’s specific data points, select the mouse pointer on the plot. [ATTACHMENT filename='simscape_results_explorer_connectedTanks.png']
14. To change the default value or initial value parameters of the model, double-click the blocks in the ConnectedTanks Simulink model. A Block Parameters dialog opens where you can change parameters. Repeat steps from 5 to 12 to run the simulation with (new) block parameters. [ATTACHMENT filename='fluiReservoir2_block_prameters.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272736199 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 03200: (2026x) Hydraulics example to Simulink and Simscape model R2016a

- page_id: `272736199`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272736199/2026x+Hydraulics+example+to+Simulink+and+Simscape+model+R2016a
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:46:52.332+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x) Exporting to External Simulation Models > (2026x) Simulink export > (2026x) Simulating exported Simulink models > (2026x) Hydraulics example to Simulink and Simscape model
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To generate a Simulink model directly from the modeling tool

1. Right-click the *Block* **Data:ConnectedTanks** and select **Tools**> **Export to Simulink.**
2. Set the options listed below and click **OK**:
  - **Format**: XML (.sdl)
  - **S-Function or Simscape**: Simscape
  - **Simscape port libraries**: Create new port types
3. Launch Matlab with Simulink and Simscape extensions.
4. In Matlab’s Current Directory navigation bar, search for the file directory where the Simulink/Simscape files (as well as the SysML v1 files) are saved, and set it as the current directory. The Current Folder panel should display the generated files.
5. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
6. In the **Current Folder** panel, double-click on the folder named**+ConnectedTanksLibrary_create** (this sets the current directory to this folder). Type **ssc_build** in the**Command Window** and press Enter. This will generate a folder **sscprj** in the **+ConnectedTanksLibrary_create** directory as well as a **ConnectedTanksLibrary_create_lib.slx** file in the directory with the original **ConnectedTanks.slx** file. Next, once again, search for and go back to the file directory where the Simulink/Simscape files (as well as the SysML files) are saved, and set it as the current directory. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. In the **Current Folder** panel, double-click on **ConnectedTanks.slx** to open the Simulink/Simscape model. Rearrange the blocks to get a better picture of the blocks and links. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
8. Go to **Simulation** > **Model Configuration Settings**. This allows to select the types of solver and the runtime of the simulation. Select a **Start Time** of **0**, and a **Stop Time** of **200** (or any other reasonable number of seconds). Change the **Max step size** to **0.01**. Under **Solver Options**, keep the solver **Type** to **Variable-step** (or any other desirable solver that is suitable). Press **Apply**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
9. In Model Configuration Settings, select **Simscape** in the**Commonly Used Parameters** panel. Change **Log simulation data** to **All**. Make sure**Limit data points**is unchecked. Press **Apply**. Press **OK**. [ATTACHMENT filename='Model_Configuration_Settings.png']
10. In the ConnectedTanks Simulink window, go to Simulation > Run .
11. Once the model has compiled, go to the Matlab’s command window and type/enter ssc_explore(simlog) (make sure that either the version of Matlab has the function ssc_explore , or a file ssc_explore.m is in the same directory as ConnectedTanks.slx in the Current Folder panel). A Simscape data logging explorer: Connected Tanks window will open up. [ATTACHMENT filename='Ssc_explore(simlog)_command_and_the_Simscape_data_logging_explorer.png']
12. In the Simscape data logging explorer , the ConnectedTanks blocks can be expanded to see how their properties simulated through time. For the tanks fluidReservoir1 and fluidReservoir2 , the properties fluidLevel can be selected to see how the fluid reacted transferred from one tank to the other in the simulation. To see the simulation’s specific data points, the Data cursor icon needs to be selected and points on the plot need to be chosen (by clicking it). [ATTACHMENT filename='Using_the_Simscape_data_loggng_explorer.png']
13. To change the default value or initial value parameters of the model, double-click the blocks in the ConnectedTanks Simulink model. A window will pop out for block parameters that can be changed. Run through Steps 7 through 10 to run the simulation with (new) block parameters again. [ATTACHMENT filename='Changing_block_parameters.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><br /></p><p>To generate a Simulink model directly from the modeling tool </p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Right-click the <em>Block</em> <strong>Data:ConnectedTanks</strong> and select <strong>Tools </strong>&gt; <strong>Export to Simulink.</strong></span><br /></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Set the options listed below and click <strong>OK</strong>:</span><ul><li><p><strong>Format</strong>: XML (.sdl)</p></li><li><p><strong>S-Function or Simscape</strong>: Simscape</p></li><li><p><strong>Simscape port libraries</strong>: Create new port types</p></li></ul></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Launch Matlab with Simulink and Simscape extensions. </span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In Matlab’s</span><strong> Current Directory</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> navigation bar, search for the file directory where the Simulink/Simscape files (as well as the SysML v1 files) are saved, and set it as the current directory. The </span><strong>Current Folder</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> panel should display the generated files. </span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image ac:width="800"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Change_Directory_in_Matlab.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Hydraulics example to Simulink and Simscape model R2016a" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br /></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the <strong>Current Folder</strong> panel, double-click on the folder named<strong> +ConnectedTanksLibrary_create</strong> (this sets the current directory to this folder). Type <strong>ssc_build</strong> in the<strong> Command Window</strong> and press Enter. This will generate a folder <strong>sscprj</strong> in the <strong>+ConnectedTanksLibrary_create</strong> directory as well as a <strong>ConnectedTanksLibrary_create_lib.slx</strong> file in the directory with the original <strong>ConnectedTanks.slx</strong> file. Next, once again, search for and go back to the file directory where the Simulink/Simscape files (as well as the SysML files) are saved, and set it as the current directory.</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image ac:width="800"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Using_the_Scc_Build_Command.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Hydraulics example to Simulink and Simscape model R2016a" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br /></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the <strong>Current Folder</strong> panel, double-click on <strong>ConnectedTanks.slx</strong> to open the Simulink/Simscape model. Rearrange the blocks to get a better picture of the blocks and links.<br /></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ConnectedTanks_Simulink_Simscape_Diagram.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Hydraulics example to Simulink and Simscape model R2016a" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br /></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Go to <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Model Configuration Settings</strong>. This allows to select the types of solver and the runtime of the simulation. Select a <strong>Start Time</strong> of <strong>0</strong>, and a <strong>Stop Time</strong> of <strong>200</strong> (or any other reasonable number of seconds). Change the <strong>Max step size</strong> to <strong>0.01</strong>. Under <strong>Solver Options</strong>, keep the solver <strong>Type</strong> to <strong>Variable-step</strong> (or any other desirable solver that is suitable). Press <strong>Apply</strong>.<br /></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image ac:width="800"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Simulink_Simscape_Model_Configuration_Settings.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Hydraulics example to Simulink and Simscape model R2016a" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br /></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In Model Configuration Settings, select <strong>Simscape</strong> in the<strong> Commonly Used Parameters</strong> panel. Change <strong>Log simulation data</strong> to <strong>All</strong>. Make sure<strong> Limit data points </strong>is unchecked. Press <strong>Apply</strong>. Press <strong>OK</strong>.</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br /></span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Model_Configuration_Settings.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Hydraulics example to Simulink and Simscape model R2016a" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>In the <strong>ConnectedTanks</strong> Simulink window, go to <strong>Simulation</strong> &gt; <strong>Run</strong>.</li><li>Once the model has compiled, go to the Matlab’s command window and type/enter<strong> ssc_explore(simlog)</strong> (make sure that either the version of Matlab has the function <strong>ssc_explore</strong>, or a file <strong>ssc_explore.m</strong> is in the same directory as <strong>ConnectedTanks.slx</strong> in the <strong>Current Folder</strong> panel). A <strong>Simscape data logging explorer: Connected Tanks</strong> window will open up.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:width="650"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ssc_explore(simlog)_command_and_the_Simscape_data_logging_explorer.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Hydraulics example to Simulink and Simscape model R2016a" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>In the <strong>Simscape data logging explorer</strong>, the <strong>ConnectedTanks</strong> blocks can be expanded to see how their properties simulated through time. For the tanks <strong>fluidReservoir1</strong> and<strong> fluidReservoir2</strong>, the properties <strong>fluidLevel</strong> can be selected to see how the fluid reacted transferred from one tank to the other in the simulation. To see the simulation’s specific data points, the <strong>Data cursor</strong> icon needs to be selected and points on the plot need to be chosen (by clicking it).<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Using_the_Simscape_data_loggng_explorer.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Hydraulics example to Simulink and Simscape model R2016a" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>To change the default value or initial value parameters of the model, double-click the blocks in the <strong>ConnectedTanks</strong> Simulink model. A window will pop out for block parameters that can be changed. Run through Steps 7 through 10 to run the simulation with (new) block parameters again.<br /><ac:image ac:width="750"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Changing_block_parameters.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Hydraulics example to Simulink and Simscape model R2016a" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br /></p>
````

**Setup for exporting into Modelica/Simulink:**

Once the*Hydraulics.mdzip*file is opened, the model should already be opened to the*ConnectedTanks*internal block diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

````html
<p><strong>Setup for exporting into Modelica/Simulink:</strong></p><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Once the </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Hydraulics.mdzip </em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">file is opened, the model should already be opened to the </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">ConnectedTanks</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> internal block diagram.</span></p><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="connected_tanks_ibd.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Hydraulics sample" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span></p><p />
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272735956 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 03202: (2026x) Hyperlinks in texts

- page_id: `272735956`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272735956/2026x+Hyperlinks+in+texts
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-11-25T14:42:51.044+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Working with model elements > (2026x) Manage Navigation > (2026x) Defining hyperlinks
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can link text directly to internal or external resources by adding hyperlinks. This enables you to open a document, wizard, diagram, or other [hyperlink type](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Edit+Hyperlink+dialog#EditHyperlinkdialog-Hyperlinktypes) by clicking the hyperlink.

##### Places to add hyperlink in text

You can add a hyperlink in the following places:

- In all elements of the text format (e.g. Note, Anchor, Comment).
- In all element property values of the text format (e.g. **To Do**, **Documentation**, **Body**, **Body** **and Language**, **Text**,**Min**,**Max**). You can insert a hyperlink only for LiteralString and OpaqueExpression [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Value Specification' space='MED'] (e.g **Default Value** property, **Value** property, and **Argument** property values).
- In all element [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification window' space=''] > Documentation/Hyperlinks property groups.

##### Adding hyperlinks in text

Before adding a hyperlink, select the text format, either HTML or Plain text. If you select:

- HTML text - you can add any type of hyperlink . >]]>
- Plain text - you can add a hyperlink only to an element/symbol. >]]>

###### all typesAdding a hyperlink in text to any hyperlink type

To add a hyperlink in text to any hyperlink type

1. Select a place to add a hyperlink. [All places to add hyperlinks in text >>](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Hyperlinks+in+texts#Hyperlinksintexts-Placestoinserthyperlinkintext)
2. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='insert_hyperlink.png'] or press Ctrl+K.
3. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Edit Hyperlink dialog' space=''] , select the tab according to hyperlink type.
4. Specify the boxes and options in the selected tab. Descriptions of boxes and tabsPlease see the following for more in-depth information about the boxes and options on each tab: 
 - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Address tab' space='']**Address** tab. 
 - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) File tab' space='']**File** tab. 
 - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Element Symbol tab' space='']**Element/Symbol** tab. 
 - [**OSLC Link** tab.](https://docs.nomagic.com/CDH/?contextKey=linking-model-elements-to-oslc-resources&version=2026x)
5. Click **OK**.The hyperlink added in text becomes underlined and blue. For example, the figure below shows where the hyperlink on the text "Car" will open the Specification window of the *Car* Class. The hyperlinked text will be represented with icon and will be automatically updated after changes. [IMAGE alt='' src='']The Element/Symbol tab in the Edit Hyperlink dialog where the text "Car" refers to the Car Class from the model. It is represented with an icon and will be automatically updated after changes.

###### elementAdding hyperlink in text to element/symbol

Two additional ways to add a hyperlink in text to an element/symbol include:

- 
- 

****

###### **drag**Adding hyperlink to element by dragging element on Note or Text box shape

This method of adding hyperlinks in text is useful only when inserting a hyperlink to element on Note or Text box shapes.

To add a hyperlink to an element by dragging an element on a Note or Text box shape

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Model Browser' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification window' space=''] , select one or more elements.
2. Drag the selected elements onto the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Note' space='MED'] or the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Text Box' space=''] shapes on the diagram pane. 
The hyperlink to the element Specification window is created on the Note or Text Box. The text become underlined and blue. The **Update Mode** and **Text to display** values are selected according default settings. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Default settings of hyperlinks to model elements' space='']How to change the default settings of hyperlinks to model elements >>

###### **guards**Adding hyperlinks to elements on Guard expressions, Change Event expressions, and State Invariant

This method of adding hyperlinks in text is useful when inserting hyperlinks to properties on:

- A Transition Guard in a State Machine diagram.
- A Flow Guard in an Activity diagram.
- A State Invariant in a Sequence diagram.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Assigning Event Type' space='MED']

To add a hyperlink on a guard expression, State Invariant, and Change Event

1. Select one of the following:
  - guard expression on Transition in State Machine diagram.
  - guard expression on Flow in Activity diagram.
  - State Invariant in Sequence diagram.
  - Change Event expression in State Machine diagram.
2. Press Ctrl+Space or Ctrl+****Backspace. If you press Ctrl+K, the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Edit Hyperlink dialog' space=''] opens and you can insert a hyperlink to an appropriate property. Makesure the selected property is correct.
3. Select the property from the list. The list provides all parameters, structural members of behavior, and structural members of the contextual classifier of behavior. The hyperlinked property is added according to the default setting of the **Update Mode** and **Text to display** list boxes. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Default settings of hyperlinks to model elements' space='']How to change the default settings of hyperlinks to model elements >> For example, in the figure below, the hyperlink to the *currentSpeed* Property is added on the guard expression. It is updated after renaming the *currentSpeed* Property to *speed*.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The hyperlink to the currentSpeed Property is added on the guard expression. It is updated after renaming the currentSpeed Property to speed in the Model Browser.

**Related pages**

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Defining hyperlinks' space='']

- Linking model elements to OSLC resources
- Managing hyperlinks
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) HTML editor' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

on Transition in State Machine diagram.</li><li>guard expression on Flow in Activity diagram.</li><li>State Invariant in Sequence diagram.</li><li>Change Event expression in State Machine diagram. </li></ul></li><li data-uuid="91d0e608-83af-426d-90d4-98261e297d7a"><p class="auto-cursor-target">Press Ctrl+Space or Ctrl+<strong> </strong>Backspace.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="57e731a0-9fbf-4ba6-94c2-467137bc7b40"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you press Ctrl+K, the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Edit Hyperlink dialog" /></ac:link> opens and you can insert a hyperlink to an appropriate property. Make<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> sure the selected property is correct.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li data-uuid="0f444c60-8723-4aaa-a59d-8a6379757dbb"><p class="auto-cursor-target">Select the property from the list.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e6f19df2-7086-4d4b-bd80-70b5a3f59e16"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The list provides all parameters, structural members of behavior, and structural members of the contextual classifier of behavior.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">The hyperlinked property is added according to the default setting of the <span class="current"><strong>Update Mode</strong> and <strong>Text to display</strong> list boxes</span>. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Default settings of hyperlinks to model elements" /><ac:link-body>How to change the default settings of <span class="current">hyperlinks to model elements</span> &gt;&gt;</ac:link-body></ac:link></p><p class="auto-cursor-target">For example, in the figure below, the hyperlink to the <em>currentSpeed</em> Property is added on the guard expression. It is updated after renaming the <em>currentSpeed</em> Property to <em>speed</em>.</p></li></ol><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="updated_hyperlink_on_guard_expression.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Hyperlinks in texts" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The hyperlink to the currentSpeed Property is added on the guard expression. It is updated after renaming the currentSpeed Property to speed in the Model Browser.</h6><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="054a0b80-eea1-4440-8572-ed2b18831aaa"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Defining hyperlinks" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><ul><li data-uuid="1243e9e8-854b-446f-8172-c01b89eb11c6"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/CDH/?contextKey=linking-model-elements-to-oslc-resources&amp;version=2026x">Linking model elements to OSLC resources</a></li><li data-uuid="4035b4a7-2a01-4c08-a32d-af05c3ff8ca5"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/DEVG/?contextKey=managing-hyperlinks&amp;version=2026x">Managing hyperlinks</a></li><li data-uuid="01ec0f38-3881-4adb-a35d-9718c78b2e3e"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) HTML editor" /></ac:link></li></ul>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272735952 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 03203: (2026x) Hyperlinks on elements

- page_id: `272735952`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272735952/2026x+Hyperlinks+on+elements
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-11-25T14:42:51.886+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Working with model elements > (2026x) Manage Navigation > (2026x) Defining hyperlinks
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Hyperlinked elements or diagrams allow you to quickly navigate through the model, to other resources, or between them. If an element or diagram refers to the , you can access that content by double clicking an element or an entire diagram. You can add all [types of hyperlinks](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Edit+Hyperlink+dialog#EditHyperlinkdialog-Hyperlinktypes) on elements and diagrams.

##### Adding a hyperlink on an element or entire diagram

To add a hyperlink on an element or entire diagram

1. Select the element or diagram.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Click the smart manipulator [IMAGE alt='' src='']**Go To** > **Manage Navigation**.
  - From the shortcut menu, choose **Go To** > **Manage Navigation**.
3. The **Specification** window opens. Choose the **Navigation/Hyperlinks** property group, and click the **Add** button.
4. In the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Edit Hyperlink dialog' space='']**Edit Hyperlink** dialog, select a tab according to the [hyperlink type](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Edit+Hyperlink+dialog#EditHyperlinkdialog-Hyperlinktypes) you want to insert.
5. Specify the boxes and options according to the selected tab. The meaning of boxes and options of each tab is described in the following pages: 
 - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Address tab' space='']**Address** tab 
 - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) File tab' space='']**File** tab 
 - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Element Symbol tab' space='']**Element/Symbol** tab 
 - [**OSLC Link** tab](https://docs.nomagic.com/CDH/?contextKey=linking-model-elements-to-oslc-resources&version=2026x)The **Text to display** box does not exist in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Edit Hyperlink dialog' space='']**Edit Hyperlink** dialog.
6. Select the **Active** check box to activate the hyperlink. Only one hyperlink can be active. By default the last added hyperlink is active. You can also:
  - Click the **Open** button, to open the content of the hyperlink, e.g., a webpage or another external resource, a file on your filesystem, or an element/symbol.
  - Click the **Edit** button, to edit an existing hyperlink in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Edit Hyperlink dialog' space='']**Edit Hyperlink** dialog.
  - Click the **Add** button, to add a new hyperlink in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Edit Hyperlink dialog' space='']**Edit Hyperlink** dialog.
  - Click the **Remove** button, to delete the hyperlink. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. Click **OK**. 
The hyperlink is added on the selected element or diagram. In the Model Browser, you will see the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Hyperlinks/Go To icon on the element. On the diagram pane, on the symbol, you will see the icon representing the subject the hyperlink is added to. You can instantly open a hyperlink by double clicking an element or diagram.

**Related pages**

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Defining hyperlinks' space='']

- Linking model elements to OSLC resources
- Managing hyperlinks

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272730446 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 03204: (2026x) Identifying Package Dependencies

- page_id: `272730446`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272730446/2026x+Identifying+Package+Dependencies
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:37:14.817+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Model analysis
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In a model, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='MED'] can be associated with other packages or model elements in a project scope or outside the project scope. In other words, packages can have relations with external elements in used projects, shared libraries, profiles, etc. An element depends on the used project when at least one of its metaproperties reference elements from at least one share of that used project. In this case, the element depends on that used project.

To avoid any violations on editing a package, especially on removing or exporting a package to another project, it is always good to know all of the package dependencies. Dependency analysis allows users to detect cyclic dependencies that impede reusing the package in the current or other projects because it can cause an infinite recursion. This analysis manages the impact of changes to other related elements.

In the modeling tool, the Dependency Checker allows you to analyze package dependencies in the whole project scope or analyze dependencies between the selected package/model and shared packages. The dependency severity level status shows how a package depends on a particular element. There are three severity levels:

- Info . The lowest severity.
- Warning . Conflicts that require your attention are displayed.
- Error . The highest severity.

#### NOTE: Severity levels

Severity levels

While checking dependencies, conflicts of the selectedseverity (and higher) are displayed in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Package Dependencies panel' space='']**Package Dependencies** panel.

In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Setting project options' space='']**Project Options** dialog, you can define the default values for checking dependencies. You can specify the following:

- The Check for Cyclic Dependencies among Used Projects option. Click to select true if you want the cyclic dependencies to be checked while exporting or sharing the packages.
- The Dependency Checker Severity Level option. Specify the severity level. The dependencies of the specified severity (and higher) will be displayed.
- The Ignore Standard/System Profiles option. Click to select true if you want to exclude the standard/system profiles from checking for cyclic dependencies in used projects.

Each time you start the dependency checking, you can change the default settings.

In the following procedures, learn how to work with the Dependency Checker:

- 
- 
- 
- 

setDefaultTo set default values for the Dependency Checker

1. On the main menu, click Options > Project . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. Expand the General option in the option group list.
3. Click the **Dependency Checker** option. The list of options opens.
4. In the Dependency Checker options list, set the desired values and click **OK** when you are done.

#### NOTE: Description of an option

Description of an option

Each option has a short description. Click the option to see the description in the description area of the dialog.

analyzeWholeTo analyze package dependencies of the whole project or between the selected package/model and shared packages

1. Choose one of the following:
  - To analyze package dependencies of the whole project, on the main menu, click **Analyze** > **Dependency Checker**.
  - To analyze dependencies between the selected package/model and shared packages, right-click the package or model in the Model Browser or on the diagram pane, point to **Tools**and then click**Dependency Checker**.
2. The Dependency Checker dialog opens. Select the Minimal Severity from the list, check ignore or not standard and system profiles while checking and click OK to execute the dependency checker. If some dependencies are found, they are displayed in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Package Dependencies panel' space='']**Package Dependencies** panel .

analyzeExportTo identify package dependencies while exporting packages

1. From the package/model shortcut menu, choose Project Usages > Export Packages to New Project . The Export Packages to New Project dialog appears.
2. Select the packages you want to export. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Exporting packages to new projects' space=''] .
3. Click OK .
4. In the question dialog, click **Yes**. NotesDependencies between the selected package/model and shared packages will be analyzed and shown in the opened[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Package Dependencies panel' space='']**Package Dependency** panel.If you want to discover *cyclic dependencies*, select the **Check for cyclic dependencies among used projects** check box. This enables for the dependencies which have **Error** and **Warning** severity levels to be displayed.

analyzeSharingTo identify package dependencies while sharing packages

1. From the package/model shortcut menu, choose Project Usages > Share Packages . The Shared Packages dialog appears.
2. Select the packages you want to share. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Sharing project data' space=''].
3. Click OK .
4. In the question dialog, click **Yes**. NotesDependencies between the selected package/model and shared packages will be analyzed and shown in the opened [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Package Dependencies panel' space='']**Package Dependency** panel.If you want to discover *cyclic dependencies*, select the **Check for cyclic dependencies among used projects** check box. This enables for the dependencies which have**Error**and**Warning**severity levels to be displayed.

#### INFO: Cyclic dependency

Cyclic dependency

If there is a chain of dependencies such that A → M(1), M(1) → M(2), M(2) → M(3), ..., M(X) → M(A), where:

- A is an element from the used project M(A)
- M(1..X) are other used projects
- A → M(x) is element A dependency on used project M(x)
- M(y) → M(x) is a dependency of at least one element in used project M(y) on used project M(x),

then this chain is called a cyclic dependency and every atomic dependency in this chain is considered as part of cyclic dependency.

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

In other words, packages can have relations with external elements in used projects, shared libraries, profiles, etc. An element depends on the used project when at least one of its metaproperties reference elements from at least one share of that used project. In this case, the element depends on that used project.</p><p>To avoid any violations on editing a package, especially on removing or exporting a package to another project, it is always good to know all of the package dependencies. Click the option to see the description in the description area of the dialog.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color: rgb(255,0,0);"><br /></span></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f0c22bfb-bfa0-4e26-8fff-ea26299092c6"><ac:parameter ac:name="">analyzeWhole</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To analyze package dependencies of the whole project or between the selected package/model and shared packages</p><hr /><ol><li>Choose one of the following:<ul><li><p>To analyze package dependencies of the whole project, on the main menu, click <strong>Analyze</strong> &gt; <strong>Dependency Checker</strong>.</p></li><li><p>To analyze dependencies between the selected package/model and shared packages, right-click the package or model in the Model Browser or on the diagram pane, point to <strong>Tools </strong>and then click<strong> Dependency Checker</strong>.</p></li></ul></li><li>The <strong>Dependency Checker</strong> dialog opens. Select the <strong>Minimal Severity</strong> from the list, check ignore or not standard and system profiles while checking and click <strong>OK </strong>to execute the dependency checker. If some dependencies are found, they are displayed in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Package Dependencies panel" /><ac:link-body><strong>Package Dependencies</strong> panel</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</li></ol><h4><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><br /></span></h4><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a6fbddf9-c38f-4e51-b775-d1375adca93e"><ac:parameter ac:name="">analyzeExport</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To identify package dependencies while exporting packages</span></p><hr /><ol><li>From the package/model shortcut menu, choose <strong>Project Usages</strong> &gt; <strong>Export Packages to New Project</strong>. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Export Packages to New Project</strong> dialog appears.</li><li>Select the packages you want to export. For more information, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Exporting packages to new projects" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Exporting packages to new projects]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li><p>In the question dialog, click <strong>Yes</strong>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4ed83e0a-d035-4270-bf19-7144a508c3e6"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Notes</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Dependencies between the selected package/model and shared packages will be analyzed and shown in the opened<ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Package Dependencies panel" /><ac:link-body> <strong>Package Dependency</strong> panel</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>If you want to discover <em>cyclic dependencies</em>, select the <strong>Check for cyclic dependencies among used projects</strong> check box. This enables for the dependencies which have <strong>Error</strong> and <strong>Warning</strong> severity levels to be displayed.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5f07b3ff-006b-4bd1-9311-449cc930d658"><ac:parameter ac:name="">analyzeSharing</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To identify package dependencies while sharing packages</span></p><hr /><ol><li>From the package/model shortcut menu, choose <strong>Project Usages</strong> &gt; <strong>Share Packages</strong>. The <strong>Shared Packages</strong> dialog appears. </li><li><p>Select the packages you want to share. For more information, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Sharing project data" /></ac:link>.</p></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li><p>In the question dialog, click <strong>Yes</strong>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3dbff1aa-82b9-49bf-8f0f-67fd53c9331d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Notes</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Dependencies between the selected package/model and shared packages will be analyzed and shown in the opened <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Package Dependencies panel" /><ac:link-body><strong>Package Dependency</strong> panel</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>If you want to discover <em>cyclic dependencies</em>, select the <strong>Check for cyclic dependencies among used projects</strong> check box. <span>This enables for the dependencies which have </span><strong>Error</strong><span> and </span><strong>Warning</strong><span> severity levels to be displayed.</span></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="36b8cd71-2b36-479a-aa43-86de22bc15ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Cyclic dependency</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If there is a chain of dependencies such that A → M(1), M(1) → M(2), M(2) → M(3), ..., M(X) → M(A), where:</p><ul><li>A is an element from the used project M(A)</li><li>M(1..X) are other used projects</li><li>A → M(x) is element A dependency on used project M(x)</li><li>M(y) → M(x) is a dependency of at least one element in used project M(y) on used project M(x),</li></ul><p>then this chain is called a cyclic dependency and every atomic dependency in this chain is considered as part of cyclic dependency.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f8f1e285-e27f-4a7e-a0fa-517801cea20e"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p> <ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="adb780d4-8338-4982-a517-dcadef4fb11e" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272729814 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 03205: (2026x) Identifying relationships

- page_id: `272729814`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272729814/2026x+Identifying+relationships
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:35:48.462+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Data-related modeling > (2026x) Transformations > (2026x) ER to SQL (generic / Oracle) transformations
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Identifying relationships are transformed in the same way as in the UML to SQL transformation, that is, the foreign key of the transformation gets to be included into the primary key of the dependent entity (the one at the multiple end of the relationship). The difference in ER to SQL transformation case is that the ER model eliminates guessing, which relationships are identifying and which ones are not. UML to SQL transformation guesses, which UML associations should be identifying, by using a heuristic method - composition associations are treated as identifying (this heuristic is controlled by the **Treat compositions as identifying**transformation property). In ER models, identifying relationships are explicitly marked as such, hence there is no need to guess (See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Identifying relationships and dependent entities' space=''] that specifies how identifying relationships is modeled).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Identifying relationships are transformed in the same way as in the UML to SQL transformation, that is, the foreign key of the transformation gets to be included into the primary key of the dependent entity (the one at the multiple end of the relationship). The difference in ER to SQL transformation case is that the ER model eliminates guessing, which relationships are identifying and which ones are not. UML to SQL transformation guesses, which UML associations should be identifying, by using a heuristic method - composition associations are treated as identifying (this heuristic is controlled by the <strong>Treat compositions as identifying </strong>transformation property). In ER models, identifying relationships are explicitly marked as such, hence there is no need to guess (See <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Identifying relationships and dependent entities" /></ac:link> that specifies how identifying relationships is modeled).</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272739342 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 03206: (2026x) Identifying relationships and dependent entities

- page_id: `272739342`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272739342/2026x+Identifying+relationships+and+dependent+entities
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:51:18.443+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Data-related modeling > (2026x) Entity-relationship (ER) modeling and diagrams
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

One-to-many and (very rarely) one-to-one relationships can be declared as “identifying” relationships. An identifying relationship is a “stronger” version of the relationship, indicating that the entity at the multiple end of the relationship cannot exist without the entity on the other end.

You can create these relationships using the buttons on the diagram pallet, as well as turn an existing relationship into an identifying one and back again. To do this, either change the **Is Identifying**property value in the relationship Specification window or select the appropriate check box on its shortcut menu.

An identifying relationship is drawn as a solid line. Non-identifying relationships use heavy dashes.

Dependent / independent entities are a closely related concept. Dependent entities occur at the multiple end of the identifying relationship. They cannot exist without the independent entity at the other end. In addition, every inherited entity (if you are doing ER modeling) is considered to be dependent. A dependent entity's primary key includes the other entity's key as part. This is implied, not shown in the model. Dependent entities are automatically recognized and drawn with rounded corners.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of identifying relationship and dependent entity in the ER diagram.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>One-to-many and (very rarely) one-to-one relationships can be declared as “identifying” relationships. An identifying relationship is a “stronger” version of the relationship, indicating that the entity at the multiple end of the relationship cannot exist without the entity on the other end.</p><p>You can create these relationships using the buttons on the diagram pallet, as well as turn an existing relationship into an identifying one and back again. To do this, either change the <strong>Is Identifying </strong>property value in the relationship Specification window or select the appropriate check box on its shortcut menu.</p><p>An identifying relationship is drawn as a solid line. Non-identifying relationships use heavy dashes.</p><p>Dependent / independent entities are a closely related concept. Dependent entities occur at the multiple end of the identifying relationship. They cannot exist without the independent entity at the other end. In addition, every inherited entity (if you are doing ER modeling) is considered to be dependent. A dependent entity's primary key includes the other entity's key as part. This is implied, not shown in the model. Dependent entities are automatically recognized and drawn with rounded corners.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2017-9-8_16-26-22.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Identifying relationships and dependent entities" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of identifying relationship and dependent entity in the ER diagram.</h6><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272737929 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 03207: (2026x) Image Library tab

- page_id: `272737929`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272737929/2026x+Image+Library+tab
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:49:24.009+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Getting started > (2026x) Understanding the user interface
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### EXCERPT: Image formats

INLINE

The**Image Library** tab displays images grouped in logical sets. You can use a bundled image collection or add your own set of images.

To open the **Image Library** tab

- From the Window menu, select Image Library. The Image Library tab opens on the right side of the window.

To add your own set of images into **Image Library**, do either

- Compress a folder (.zip) with a set of desired images and place it in the modeling tool's installationdirectory: *\data\imagelibraries\commonsymbols*. Image formatsYou can add images of the following formats: .gif, .jpg, .jpeg, .svg, .png, .wmf.
- Create an Image Library plugin. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Writing plugins' space='DEVG'] page.

Using the **Image Library** tab, you can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Applying image' space=''].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The **Image Library** tab consist of three components:

##### Quick filter

You can search for images in the **Whole hierarchy** or **Recently used** tabs using one of the following way:

- Simple Search: type what you want to find in the hierarchy.
- Pattern Search: type * to find any image. For example, type "*Bank" if you want to find "Bank" somewhere in the hierarchy.

##### Image Library tree

You can drag an image from the **Image Library** tree directly:

- On the symbol (image is applied for symbol).
- To the diagram pane (image shape is created).

##### Image Library Preview area

You can preview the image before applying it on the symbol in the **Image Library** Preview area.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

## PAGE 03208: (2026x) Image Shape

- page_id: `272737848`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272737848/2026x+Image+Shape
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:49:19.861+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Auxiliary diagram symbols
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

490233696

490233698

490233697

The image shape provides a simple and quick way to insert a image into a diagram. Images may include logos, graphs, tables, or others. The default shape size after the insertion is the actual image size.

You can format image shape representation properties in the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Formatting symbols' space='']**Symbol Properties**dialog.

To insert an image on the diagram pane

1. On the diagram palette, in the Common category, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2017-06-28 at 12.58.27 PM.png'] Image Shape button or press the I key.
2. Click on the diagram pane. The Open dialog opens.
3. Browse for an image in the *.gif, *.jpg, *.jpeg, *.svg, or *.png format and click Open .

490233695

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Auxiliary diagram symbols' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Diagramming' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272735756 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 03209: (2026x) Image tool

- page_id: `272735756`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272735756/2026x+Image+tool
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:45:48.133+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Report Wizard > (2026x) Helper tools
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1267909359

1267909360

1267909360

$image provides a rich set of image manipulation methods that enable you to transform images during report generation. With **$image**, you can scale, rotate, split, and resize images, include external images, and export them for TXT and HTML templates.

**Syntax**

These manipulation methods can be broadly divided into seven categories:

1. Scaling images
2. Rotating images
3. Splitting images
4. Resizing fixed-pixel images
5. Resizing fixed-measurement images
6. Including external images
7. Exporting images for TXT and HTML templates
8. Limiting image size

Multiple image transformations will have cumulative effects. Desired sizes can be specified either as pixel counts or as measurements in inches, centimeters, or millimeters. While the parameters for resizing an image with a specific measurement (in, cm, mm, pt, or px) must be specified in a pair of quote marks, all other size parameters do not need to be specified in a pair of quote marks.

The **keepRatio** parameter takes either a true or false value; a true value will resize the image and keep the original image height/width ratio; a false value will only resize one axis, as set by the method name, resulting in a stretched image.

1267909358

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

With <strong>$image</strong>, you can scale, rotate, split, and resize images, include external images, and export them for TXT and HTML templates.</p><p><strong>Syntax</strong></p><p>These manipulation methods can be broadly divided into seven categories:</p><ol><li>Scaling images</li><li>Rotating images</li><li>Splitting images</li><li>Resizing fixed-pixel images</li><li>Resizing fixed-measurement images</li><li>Including external images</li><li>Exporting images for TXT and HTML templates</li><li>Limiting image size</li></ol><p>Multiple image transformations will have cumulative effects. Desired sizes can be specified either as pixel counts or as measurements in inches, centimeters, or millimeters. While the parameters for resizing an image with a specific measurement (in, cm, mm, pt, or px) must be specified in a pair of quote marks, all other size parameters do not need to be specified in a pair of quote marks.</p><p>The <strong>keepRatio</strong> parameter takes either a true or false value; a true value will resize the image and keep the original image height/width ratio; a false value will only resize one axis, as set by the method name, resulting in a stretched image.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f0e833e7-926f-4a24-bee8-36421e59fb28"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1267909358</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="b0bb516e-26b0-4363-a404-fddac23bdb9f" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 03210: (2026x) Impact analysis relation maps

- page_id: `272738614`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738614/2026x+Impact+analysis+relation+maps
- version_number: 1

- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) Procedures > (2026x) Managing traceability and impact analysis
- labels: ['impact-analysis-relation-maps', 'node-role-impact-analysis-map', 'node-impact-analysis-map', 'resource-role-impact-analysis-map', 'resource-impact-analysis-map', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

216907718

216907721

216907719

Take your model to the next steps - perform the impact analysis. For this purpose, there are four predefined relation maps. Using these maps, you are able to identify the model elements which are affected by the changes of the context element.

The predefined relation maps for the impact analysis are the following:

- Operational Role Impact Analysis Map
- Operational Performer Impact Analysis Map
- Resource Role Impact Analysis Map
- Resource Impact Analysis Map

Several predefined criteria helps you to determine how the context element is related to other model elements.

The Legend in the right-top corner shows you which color represents which criteria.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

216907717

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating predefined relation map' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Modifying criteria through the legend' space='']

1335007764

1335007753

###### Description

The Impact Matrix describes the mapping between the Strategic/Operational/Resource/Service Elements and Strategic Elements using the Impacted By relationship directly or using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Using Implied Relations' space=''].

###### Implementation

The rows of the matrix are:

- Operational Elements (Operational Activity and Operational Architecture)
- Resources Elements (Resource Architecture)
- Services Elements (Service Architecture)
- Strategic Elements (Actual Outcome, Capability, and Actual Strategic Phase)

The columns of the matrix are:

- Strategic Elements (Challenge, Opportunity, and Enterprise Goal)

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272739560 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 03212: (2026x) Implementation Matrix

- page_id: `272739560`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272739560/2026x+Implementation+Matrix
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:51:31.398+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) Procedures
- labels: ['implementation-matrix', 'mapping-between-systems-operational-and-service-elements', 'nodes/performers-implementation-matrix', 'operational-activities-implementation-matrix', 'operational-exchanges-implementation-matrix', 'ov-sv-gap-analysis', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1370353206

1370353209

1370353207

**Description**

The **Implementation Matrix** describes the mapping between the Resources / Operational / Summary & Overview Elements and Operational / Services / Strategic / Resources Elementsdirectly or using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Using Implied Relations' space=''].The implementation in UAF is defined by the Implements relationship. To specify the implementation and analyze implementation gaps, the Implementation Matrix is added as a supportive product for UAF.

**Implementation**

An Implementation Matrix is represented by a Dependency Matrix-based diagram.

The Rows of the matrix are:

- Resources Elements: Resource Performer, Function, Resource Interfaces, Resource Information, Resource Role, Resource Service, Resource Connector, and Resource Exchange.
- Operational Elements: Operational Activity and Operational Information.
- Service Elements: Service Interface.
- Summary & Overview Elements: Architecture.

The Columns of this matrix are:

- Operational Elements: Operational Agent, Operational Activity, Operational Interface, Operational Information, Operational Role, Operational Connector, and Operational Exchange.
- Services Elements: Service, Service Function, and Service Interface.
- Strategic Elements: Actual Strategic Phase and Strategic Information.
- Resource Elements: Resource Connector.

**Sample**

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

**Predefined configurations**

The predefined matrices are only available in DoDAF and DoDAF 2.0 perspectives.

Working in DoDAF or DoDAF 2.0 perspectives, you can find predefined matrices by clicking **Analyze** > **OV-SV Gap analysis**.

There are several types of predefined implementation matrices:

- Operational Performers Implementation Matrix. It maps Operational Performers to System Resources only.
- Operational Activities Implementation Matrix. It maps Operational Activities to Functions.
- Operational Exchanges Implementation Matrix. It maps Operational Exchanges to Resource Exchanges.

These matrices provide an analysis of the whole model of a particular implementation, so you do not have to define the scope or any additional properties for building them.

1370353205

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Function' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Exchange' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Performer' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Activity' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Exchange' space='MED']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Using Implied Relations' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272735082 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 03213: (2026x) Implementing or Overriding Operations

- page_id: `272735082`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272735082/2026x+Implementing+or+Overriding+Operations
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-11-25T14:43:01.883+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Tools
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### NOTE: Modeling Tools

Modeling Tools

This functionality is available in Standard, Professional, Architect and Enterprise editions only.

When you inherit Classes from the base Class that has abstract functions, you have to redefine them in the inherited Classes. The implement/override operations tool will help you generate operations that are defined as abstract in the base Class.

The**Implement/Override Operations**command can be invoked in two cases:

- When one classifier inherits operations from the base classifier ( [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Case study #1: Adding a rule to create a Generalization relationship on drag-and-drop' space='UPDG'] ).
- When some classifiers implement an Interface ( [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Forward traceability - realization' space=''] ).

To start the Implement/Override Operations****tool

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='DH Links panel shortcut menu and Sync Status icons' space='CDH'] of the selected Class, select Tools > Code Engineering .
2. Select Implement/Override Operations.
3. The Select Operations to Implement/Override dialog box opens.

| Box | Function |
| --- | --- |
| All | Contains names of all operations defined within the selected class. |
| Selected | Contains the selected operations. |
| > | Moves the selected operation from the All list to the Selected list. |
| < | Moves selected operation from the Selected list to the All list. |
| >> | Moves all operations from the All list to the Selected list. |
| << | Moves all operations from the Selected to the All list. |
| OK | Generates operations that are in the Selected list. |
| Cancel | Exits the dialog box without any changes. |
| Help | Displays help. |

#### TIP: Shortcut Key

Shortcut Key

Double-click the item name and it will be moved to the opposite list.

**Related Pages**

- Tools
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='MED']
- Class diagram
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Case study #1: Adding a rule to create a Generalization relationship on drag-and-drop' space='UPDG']
- Realization relationship

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272731193 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 03214: (2026x) Implied Connectors

- page_id: `272731193`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272731193/2026x+Implied+Connectors
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:38:37.083+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x) Modeling structure with Blocks > (2026x) Connecting Blocks in SysML Internal Block Diagram
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The implied relation functionality allows you to analyze existing relations among parts in the Internal Block Diagrams. It let you know that parts are somehow related with each other without giving the detailed view of actual Connectors and ports. You can display implied relations between parts which are connected through ports/interfaces. By default, the implied relation is represented as a dashed line with a short summary in label of how many actual connectors are underneath. You can specify a different style of the implied relation by using its the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Symbol Properties dialog' space='']**Symbol Properties** dialog.

The implied relation functionality allows you to:

- Display implied relations between [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part Property' space='MED'] through ports/interfaces.
- Display actual [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MED'] and ports from the implied relations level by level or all at once.
- Find actual Connectors in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Containment tab' space=''] directly from the implied relation.
- Specify [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Symbol Properties dialog' space=''] of the implied relation.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### On the left side: the implied relation shows that 18 connectors exist between TV and TV Tuner parts. On the right side: all 18 actual connectors and ports are shown by using the Semantic Zoom In, or Display Underlying Connectors command.

How to work with implied Connectors read in the following pages:
